revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/24314731306|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
[[fr:Isagawa-jinja]][[ja:率川神社]][[simple:Isagawa Shrine]] {{Infobox religious building|image=File:Isagawa-jinja, shaden, Nara.jpg|prefecture={{ill|Nara|qid=Q169134|1=Nara (city)}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.68125|N|135.82572222222223|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}|deity={{ill|Himetataraisuzuhime|qid=Q5353793|1=Himetataraisuzu-hime}}}} {{infobox shinto shrine | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | image = Isagawa-jinja, shaden, Nara.jpg |deity={{ill|Himetataraisuzu-hime|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himetataraisuzu-hime|qq=}}
Sai no Okami ({{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|qq=|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}/{{ill|Ōmononushi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=}})
{{ill|Tamakushi-hime|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamakushi-hime|qq=}} }} Isagawa Shrine, established in the year 593 under a national decree by Omiwa no Kimishiratsutsumi, stands as the oldest shrine in Nara City, Japan. The shrine is unique among Shinto shrines in Japan, being one of the rare shrines dedicated to an empress rather than an emperor or other male deity.{{Cite web|date=2023-02-04|title=About Isagawa Shrine {{!}} 率川神社(いさがわじんじゃ)|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20230204004947/https://isagawa-jinja.jp/en/jinja/|access-date=2024-04-10|website=web.archive.org}} It is dedicated to {{ill|Himetataraisuzu-hime|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himetataraisuzu-hime|qq=}}. And It is related to {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kōfuku-ji|qq=}} It is currently a massha of {{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|qid=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}}.{{Cite journal|last=Tyler|first=Royall|date=1990|title=Kōfukuji and the Mountains of Yamato|url=https://www.jstor.org/stable/25790890|journal=Japan Review|issue=1|pages=153–223|issn=0915-0986}} == Enshrined deity == The principal deity of Isagawa Shrine is Empress {{ill|Himetataraisuzu-hime|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himetataraisuzu-hime|qq=}} no Mikoto, celebrated for her wisdom and her supportive role to [[Emperor Jimmu]] during his reign. The Empress, being the wife of [[Emperor Jimmu]], is honored at the shrine alongside her parents, reflecting the shrine's theme of parental protection and the well-being of children. === The Divine Family === * Center Shrine: Empress {{ill|Himetataraisuzu-hime|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himetataraisuzu-hime|qq=}} no Mikoto * Left Shrine: Sai no Okami ({{ill|Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|qid=Q8009611|lt=Kotoshironushi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kotoshironushi|qq=|1=Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto}}/{{ill|Ōmononushi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmononushi|qq=}}), the Empress's father * Right Shrine: {{ill|Tamakushi-hime|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tamakushi-hime|qq=}} no Mikoto, the Empress's mother This arrangement symbolizes the natural instinct of parents to protect their children, a theme that resonates deeply with visitors, especially parents praying for their children's well-being. == Cultural Significance == Isagawa Shrine is a focal point for those seeking blessings for the health and safety of their children. Its historical and cultural significance extends beyond its spiritual offerings, serving as a testament to the enduring legacy of Empress Himetataraisuzuhime no Mikoto's wisdom and benevolence. == History == It is said that on February 3, 593, during the first {{ill|Empress|en|Empress|qid=Q100387742|lt=year|1=Empress}} of the reign of {{ill|Empress Suiko|en|Empress Suiko|qid=Q298057|lt=Empress|1=Empress Suiko}} Suiko, Omiwa no Kimi Hakutsutsumi enshrined the Empress Himetataraisuzuhime, the wife of {{ill|Emperor Jimmu|en|Emperor Jimmu|qid=Q200188|lt=Emperor|1=Emperor Jimmu}} Jimmu, at this shrine by imperial decree , and it is the oldest shrine in Nara City. Later, {{ill|Empress Genshō|en|Empress Genshō|qid=Q235343|lt=Empress Gensho|1=Empress Genshō}} enshrined {{ill|Himetataraisuzu-hime|lt=Himetataraisuzu-hime's|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himetataraisuzu-hime}} father, [[Ōmononushi|Sai no Okami]], on the right side of the main hall, and her mother, Tamakushihime, on the left side. In [[S.852|852]], during the reign of {{ill|Emperor Montoku|en|Emperor Montoku|qid=Q357225|lt=Emperor Montoku|1=Emperor Montoku}} {{ill|Ninju|en|Ninju|qid=Q1373077|lt=,|1=Ninju}} he was awarded {{ill|Rank|en|Rank|qid=Q4120621|lt=the Rank of|1=Rank}} Junior Fifth Rank and was given six divine titles (four in the Sakyo region and two {{ill|Tango Province|en|Tango Province|qid=Q1054331|lt=in Tango Province|1=Tango Province}} ) . In {{ill|Jishō|en|Jishō|qid=Q1194671|lt=December|1=Jishō}} {{ill|1180|en|1180|qid=Q19702|lt=1180|1=1180}}, the shrine was burned down during the {{ill|Siege of Nara|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Siege of Nara|qq=}} by {{ill|Taira no Shigehira|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taira no Shigehira}} . It was {{ill|Kenkyū|en|Kenkyū|qid=Q1193933|lt=rebuilt|1=Kenkyū}} in {{ill|1190|en|1190|qid=Q19736|lt=1190|1=1190}} with the support of [[:en:Kofukuji Temple|Kofuku-ji Temple]], and thereafter came under the control of the temple. Since {{ill|Middle Ages|en|Middle Ages|qid=Q12554|lt=the Middle Ages|1=Middle Ages}}, it has been managed by [[Kasuga-taisha|the Kasuga Wakamiya]] priests and has come to be known as "Kasuga Saegusa Shrine." For this reason, records of shrine relocations during the Middle Ages are often recorded in [[Daijo-in Temple and Shrine Notes|the Daijoin Temple and Shrine Miscellaneous Records]] . In the early modern period, it was one of the eleven shrines in the outer sanctuary of Kasuga Taisha Shrine, but in March {{ill|1877|en|1877|qid=Q7773|lt=1877|1=1877}} ( {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji|1=Meiji era}} 10), it was designated as Kawanishi-Okami Mikojinja Shrine, a branch shrine of Ogami Shrine, by an order from {{ill|Home Ministry|en|Home Ministry|qid=Q1142265|lt=the Ministry of Home Affairs|1=Home Ministry}} . The child deity ( Himetataraisuzuhime ) is enshrined in the center of the main hall, with the parent deities (the father deity Sai [[Ōmononushi|no Okami]] to the west and the mother deity [[Princess Tamakushi|Tamakushihime]] to the east) enshrined on either side. For this reason, the deity has long been praised as " '''child-soothing deity''' ," and is devoutly worshipped as the deity of safe childbirth and child-rearing . The Rate River, which flows along the southern edge, is also commonly known as the Komori River. == Precincts == * Main Hall - Three one-bay Kasuga-style shrine buildings are built side by side and connected by partitions. Each has a seventh-class wooden staircase on the front, with a balustrade decorated with [[Giboshi]] . The roof is covered with cypress bark and has a box ridge with [[千木勝男木|chigi katsuogi beams]] . Judging from the detailed construction, it is believed to have been built in the early {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} . ** Nakahonden (designated tangible cultural property by Nara Prefecture) ** Right main hall (designated tangible cultural property by Nara Prefecture) ** Left main hall (designated tangible cultural property by Nara Prefecture) * Worship hall * Otogawa Awa Shrine - See below * [[Sumiyoshi Shrine]] - Deities: [[Sumiyoshi sanjin|Kamitsutsuo, Nakatsutsuo, Sokotsutsuo, and]] {{ill|Empress Jingū|en|Empress Jingū|qid=Q232803|lt=Okinagaobihime|1=Empress Jingū}} * [[Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga Shrine]] - Deities: [[Takemikazuchi|Takemikazuchi-no-Mikoto]], Futsunushi, Ameno- [[Ameno Koyane no Mikoto|Koyane-no-Mikoto,]] [[Himegami]] * Frog Stone * Shrine office File:Isagawa_Jinja_Haiden_Nara.jpg|Worship hall File:Isagawa_Jinja_Honden_Nara.jpg|The main hall as seen from the side of the worship hall File:Isagawa_Jinja_Kahi_Nara.jpg|Man'yoshu Poetry Monument{{Refn|歌は「はね蘰（かづら） 今する妹を うら若み いざ率川の 音の清（さや）けさ」（巻七・1112番、作者未詳）|group="Note"}} File:Isagawa_Jinja_Yuzuriha_Nara.jpg|Planting Yew Trees File:Isagawa_Awa_Jinja_Torii_Nara.jpg|Sub-shrines and the Torii of Otogawa Awa Shrine File:Isagawa_Jinja_Kasugasha_Nara.jpg|Sub-shrine Kasuga Shrine File:Isagawa_Jinja_Sumiyoshi_Nara.jpg|Sub-shrine Sumiyoshi Shrine File:Isagawa_Jinja_Chouzusha_Nara.jpg|Chozuya (purification fountain) File:Isagawa_Jinja_Frog_Stone_Nara.jpg|Frog Stone File:Isagawa_Jinja_Oomiwa_Yohai_Nara.jpg|{{ill|Ōmiwa Shrine|en|Ōmiwa Shrine|qid=Q705542|lt=Ōmiwa Shrine|1=Ōmiwa Shrine}} distant {{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=worship site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho}} File:Isagawa_Jinja_Torii_Nara.jpg|Front Torii File:Isagawa_Jinja_West_Torii_Nara.jpg|Western Torii == Saegusa Festival == The annual festival, held on June 17th, is commonly known as '''the Lily Festival''' . The bamboo lilies that bloom on {{ill|Mount Miwa|en|Mount Miwa|qid=Q860627|lt=Mount Miwa|1=Mount Miwa}} were called '''saigusa''' in ancient times, and these flowers are offered to the deity. The offering is * Shiraki and [[Sake|pure sake]] are placed in a kettle (son), and kuroki and unrefined {{ill|Nigori|en|Nigori|qid=Q60995819|lt=sake|1=Nigori}} are placed in a can (hotogi), and the sake barrel is surrounded by lily stems. * The matured food is placed in a box, covered with an oak leaf, and placed on a black wood shelf. * Lilies are offered in a large bucket The offerings are made in accordance with ancient customs, and the festival, which is based on the Chinka Festival, includes prayers for warding off evil epidemics. On the day of the festival, in accordance with the ancient customs of the enshrined deity, {{ill|Himetataraisuzu-hime|4=en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himetataraisuzu-hime|qq=}}, a procession of seven young maidens carrying lilies takes place throughout the town after the ceremony. This is an old festival that is recorded in {{ill|Taihō Code|en|Taihō Code|qid=Q1059892|lt=the Taiho Code|1=Taihō Code}}, and the {{ill|Yōrō Code|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yōrō Code}} also states that "it is called the Saegusa Festival because it is a festival in which sake barrels are decorated with Saegusa flowers." == Isagawa Awa Shrine == {{Main|Isagawa Awa Shrine}} Isagawa Awa Shrine is a subshrine dedicated to [[Kotoshironushi-no-Mikoto|Kotoshironushi]]. It is located between Sumiyoshi Shrine and Kasuga Shrine, which are branch shrines of Isagawa Shrine. It is said to have been erected by {{ill|Dainagon|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dainagon|qq=}} {{ill|Fujiwara no Korekimi|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Korekimi}} in the second year of the {{ill|Hōki|en|Hōki|qid=Q18520676|lt=Hōki|1=Hōki}} era ( {{ill|771|en|771|qid=Q29950|lt=771|1=771}} ), and in the second year of {{ill|Ninju|en|Ninju|qid=Q1373077|lt=the Jinshu|1=Ninju}} era ( [[S.852|852]] ), he was awarded the Rank of {{ill|Junior Fifth Rank|ja|従五位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fifth Rank}} . A small shrine (shikinai shosha) listed in the [[Engishiki Jinmyōchō]] as " '''Isagawa Awa Shrine''' " . The enshrined deity is Kotoshironushi no Mikoto, commonly known as [[Ebisu]], and in addition to the regular festival on June 17th, the first Ebisu festival is held on January 5th . [[File:Old_Isagawa-Awa_Shrine_(NishiJodo-cho,_Nara)_2.jpg|thumb|The former site of Otogawa Awa Shrine in Nishikido-machi]] It was destroyed by a peasant uprising in {{ill|1532|en|1532|qid=Q6436|lt=1532|1=1532}} and by fire during the wars waged by the {{ill|Miyoshi Trio|ja|三好三人衆|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyoshi Trio}} and {{ill|Matsunaga Hisahide|en|Matsunaga Hisahide|qid=Q1143038|lt=Matsunaga Hisahide|1=Matsunaga Hisahide}} . According to a mid- {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} book by {{ill|Murai Kodō|ja|村井古道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Murai Kodō}} " {{nihongo|Narabō's clumsy explanation|奈良坊目拙解|Narabō Mokusetsukai}} ," the old site was located on the south side of Nishijodo-cho, behind the second house from the east, and a single {{ill|Pine|en|Pine|qid=Q12024|lt=pine tree|1=Pine}} was enshrined as a sacred tree, but it had fallen into severe disrepair. It was not until the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era}} that a small shrine was enshrined, and in {{ill|1920|en|1920|qid=Q2155|lt=1920|1=1920}} ( {{ill|Taishō era|en|Taishō era|qid=Q329174|lt=Taisho|1=Taishō era}} 9), the main shrine building was moved to the grounds of Isagawa Shrine. {{ill|1959|en|1959|qid=Q5302|lt=In 1959|1=1959}} ( {{ill|Shōwa era|en|Shōwa era|qid=Q327567|lt=Showa|1=Shōwa era}} 34), due to development of the temple grounds, it was moved to its current location along with the subordinate shrines Sumiyoshi Shrine and Kasuga Shrine. Currently, in front of the shrine building, there is a square stone lantern with the inscription " Awa Shrine, 1803 (3rd year of the Kyoho era )" that was moved from its previous location. == Visiting Isagawa Shrine == The shrine offers various traditional charms and participates in festivals that highlight its rich history and cultural importance. Visitors to the shrine can expect to experience a profound sense of peace and reverence, set amidst the backdrop of Nara's ancient beauty. === Key Features === * Omamori Charms: Available for visitors seeking blessings and protection. * Festivals: The shrine hosts annual events that celebrate its history and cultural heritage. === Accessibility === Isagawa Shrine is accessible to visitors from all over the world, with information available in multiple languages including English, Traditional Chinese, Simplified Chinese, and Korean, ensuring that its cultural and spiritual significance is shared widely. == Literary references == In his last novel " {{ill|The Sea of Fertility|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Sea of Fertility|qq=}} {{ill|Yukio Mishima|en|Yukio Mishima|qid=Q134456|lt=," Yukio Mishima|1=Yukio Mishima}} quoted the character Honda as saying about the Saegusa Festival at Isagawa Shrine, "I have never seen such a beautiful ritual."{{ill|The Sea of Fertility|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Sea of Fertility}}, {{ill|Yukio Mishima|en|Yukio Mishima|qid=Q134456|lt=Yukio Mishima|1=Yukio Mishima}} {{User:Immanuelle/Ritual ceremonies of the Imperial Palace updated}} ==Gallery== === Main shrine === File:Isagawa Jinja Chouzusha Nara.jpg|Chōzusha (Pavilion for ritual cleansing) File:Isagawa Jinja Frog Stone Nara.jpg|Frog-shaped Stone File:Isagawa Jinja Haiden Nara.jpg|Haiden (Prayer hall) File:Isagawa Jinja Honden Nara.jpg|Honden (Main shrine building) File:Isagawa Jinja Kahi Nara.jpg|Man'yōshū Memorial (Ancient poetry collection monument) File:Isagawa Jinja Kasugasha Nara.jpg|Kasugasha Shrine (Dedicated to {{ill|Kasuga-no-Kami|fr|4=simple|5=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga-no-Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kasuga-no-Kami}}) File:Isagawa Jinja Office Nara.jpg|Administrative Office File:Isagawa Jinja Oomiwa Yohai Nara.jpg|Oomiwa Yohai ({{ill|Haisho|ja|拝所|lt=Worship site|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Haisho}} dedicated to Omiwa) File:Isagawa Jinja Rest Place Nara.jpg|Resting Area File:Isagawa Jinja Sumiyoshi Nara.jpg|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Dedicated to maritime deities) File:Isagawa Jinja Torii Nara.jpg|Torii (Traditional Shinto gate) File:Isagawa Jinja West Torii Nara.jpg|West Torii File:Isagawa Jinja Yuzuriha Nara.jpg|Yuzuriha (Symbolic tree) File:Isagawa-jinja rope torii.jpg|Rope Torii File:Isagawa-jinja office.jpg|Administrative Office File:Isagawa-jinja glass shaden, Nara.jpg|Shaded Glass Structure File:Isagawa-jinja Frog Stone.jpg|Frog-shaped Stone File:Isagawa-jinja 05.jpg|Unidentified Object File:Isagawa-jinja shaden from right.jpg|View from the Right of the Shaded Structure File:Isagawa-jinja inside haiden.jpg|View across Shaded Courtyard from Haiden File:Isagawa-jinja looking through shaden.jpg|Inside the Shaded Courtyard File:Isagawa-jinja looking over wall into Shaden enclosure.jpg|Peeking into the Shaded Courtyards File:Isagawa-jinja ema.jpg|Ema (Votive plaques) Nara.jpg|The shaden === Awa Shrine === File:Isagawa Awa Jinja Nara.jpg|Awa Jinja File:Isagawa Awa Jinja Torii Nara.jpg|Awa Jinja Torii File:Isagawa Awa jinja Torii 2023.jpg|View 10 File:Isagawa Awa jinja 1.jpg|View 11 File:Isagawa Awa jinja 2.jpg|View 12 File:Isagawa Awa jinja 3.jpg|View 13 File:Isagawa Awa jinja 4.jpg|View 14 File:Isagawa Awa jinja 5.jpg|View 15 File:Isagawa-jinja, keidaisha.jpg|Keidaisha == References == {{reflist}} {{⚠️🚨COPIED from EverybodyWiki ❗❕⚠️😡😤Please respect Licence CC-BY-SA ❗}} {{Kasuga Faith}} {{translated
